The Spectrum Deal
-----------------

The Los Angeles Dodgers have become the villains of Major League Baseball,
consistently signing top free agents every off-season, because the team can=
 afford to spend more on player salaries than any other MLB club. This fina=
ncial advantage is conferred by the fact that the Dodgers have a local medi=
a deal that is impervious to the secular problems negatively impacting regi=
onal sports networks throughout the industry. Amid a time when cable distri=
butors are forcing teams to lonelier price tiers, Charter is paying the Dod=
gers an average of $334 million a year through
2038=E2=80=94the most team-friendly deal in the sport.

=C2=A0

The Lakers=E2=80=99 local deal, also with Charter, is not nearly as lucrati=
ve as the Dodgers=E2=80=99. But the team=E2=80=99s local rights fee deal wi=
th Spectrum SportsNet is also the richest in its league, and will approach =
$200 million this year, sources have told me. Like the Dodgers, the Lakers=
=E2=80=99 deal is long-term and runs until the late 2030s. The Dallas Maver=
icks,
meanwhile, are at the opposite end of the local rights spectrum. Before thi=
s season, the Mavericks walked away from a deal with Bally Sports that woul=
d have paid the team around $45 million per year.

=C2=A0

In its place, the team signed a deal with the local broadcast group, Tegna,=
 that carries games on over-the-stations in the area. Local broadcast chann=
els provide more reach than R.S.N.s, but they rarely come close to the righ=
ts
fees that R.S.N.s pay. The Mavs also started a direct-to-consumer business =
called Mavs TV, though it is not known how many subscribers have signed up =
for it. Nevertheless, sources have suggested that the Lakers local media ri=
ghts haul is seven or eight times higher than the Mavericks.

As the R.S.N. business continues to crater, the local media environments in=
 both markets certainly played a part in this deal. Luka, after all, was on=
 pace to demand a five-year $345 million deal this
summer. Sure, the NBA=E2=80=99s $77 billion national media deals will kick =
in next season, but it=E2=80=99s hard to see how the Mavs could afford to k=
eep the star Slovenian guard while countenancing the dislocation of its loc=
al rights situation. There=E2=80=99s a growing belief inside professional s=
ports circles that Cuban, who sold the bulk of the team to the Adelson fami=
ly last year, foresaw this scenario unfolding and cashed out in advance. In=
deed, when Cuban bought the team in 2000, he helped the
NBA ownership understand the financial upside of their media potential. The=
 next wave of ownership economics will likely be defined by real estate dea=
lings=E2=80=94hotels, casino development, interactive stadiums, etcetera. I=
t=E2=80=99s hardly a wonder why Cubes is out and the Adelsons are in charge=
 now.

nt into full-blown overdrive, wondering aloud on social media who knew <em>=
what</em>, and <em>when</em>.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;
margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Had =
<strong>LeBron James</strong>, whose camp signaled that he learned about th=
e deal at a post-game family dinner, actually been in on the plot? Talmudic=
 readers would note that LeBron=E2=80=99s postgame assessment of A.D., who=
=E2=80=99d been sitting out due to an injury, was a little <em>too </em>eff=
usive. Also, Lakers owner <strong>Jeanie Buss</strong> and general manager =
<strong>Rob Pelinka </strong>would never blindside their biggest star
with a personnel move of this magnitude, would they? After all, LeBron esse=
ntially picks his coaches=E2=80=94plus, his son is on the team, and was pre=
sumably a party to the ostensible postgame dinner alibi.</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;=
">Also unusual: Dallas Mavericks general manager <strong>Nico Harrison</str=
ong>, whose team reached the NBA Finals the previous season, had put out a =
hasty post-deal narrative suggesting that Davis had become his
own white whale=E2=80=94a two-way player who worked hard, conditioned his b=
ody, and fit his culture. Yes, yes, that=E2=80=99s all well and good. Indee=
d, Davis is a generational talent, a historic defender, an NBA champ, and a=
 former top pick, and he has a relationship with Harrison from the latter=
=E2=80=99s stint at Nike. Notably absent from Harrison=E2=80=99s spiel, how=
ever, were the persnickety observations that Davis has had his own injury p=
roblems and, in a past life, forced his trade to L.A. by storming his way o=
ut of New
Orleans=E2=80=94not quite culture-bearer stuff.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;"><=
br/>Anyway, these curiosities laddered up to more existential questions=E2=
=80=A6 in fact, to a very simple question: How had this happened? Superstar=
s on winning teams are <em>never</em> exchanged midseason. And generational=
 scorers like Luka=E2=80=94especially in secondary NBA markets, such as Dal=
las, and especially on winning teams=E2=80=94are prized possessions. To wit=
: Mavs owner <strong>Mark Cuban</strong>, who recently sold down his positi=
on in the
team, <em>ensured </em>that <strong>Dirk Nowitzki</strong> retired in Dalla=
s. In the end, though, the answer=E2=80=94or at least <em>part</em> of the =
answer=E2=80=94appears to be deceptively simple: media economics.</p></div>

<table width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" role=

><div style=3D"color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:'Inter',&#39=

50%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"margin:0;margi=
n-bottom:0px;">The Los Angeles Dodgers have become the villains of Major Le=
ague Baseball,
consistently signing top free agents every off-season, because the team can=
 afford to spend more on player salaries than any other MLB club. This fina=
ncial advantage is conferred by the fact that the Dodgers have a local medi=
a deal that is impervious to the secular problems negatively impacting regi=
onal sports networks throughout the industry. Amid a time when cable distri=
butors are forcing teams to lonelier price tiers, Charter is paying the Dod=
gers an average of $334 million a year <em>through
2038</em>=E2=80=94the most team-friendly deal in the sport.</p><p style=3D"=
margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0=
px;">The Lakers=E2=80=99 local deal, also with Charter, is not nearly as lu=
crative as the Dodgers=E2=80=99. But the team=E2=80=99s local rights fee de=
al with Spectrum SportsNet is also the richest in its league, and will appr=
oach $200 million this year, sources have told me. Like the Dodgers, the La=
kers=E2=80=99 deal is long-term and runs until the late 2030s. The Dallas M=
avericks,
meanwhile, are at the opposite end of the local rights spectrum. Before thi=
s season, the Mavericks walked away from a deal with Bally Sports that woul=
d have paid the team around $45 million per year.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;m=
argin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">In its place, the team =
signed a deal with the local broadcast group, Tegna, that carries games on =
over-the-stations in the area. Local broadcast channels provide more reach =
than R.S.N.s, but they rarely come close to the rights
fees that R.S.N.s pay. The Mavs also started a direct-to-consumer business =
called Mavs TV, though it is not known how many subscribers have signed up =
for it. Nevertheless, sources have suggested that the Lakers local media ri=
ghts haul is seven or eight times higher than the Mavericks.<br/><br/>As th=
e R.S.N. business continues to crater, the local media environments in both=
 markets certainly played a part in this deal. Luka, after all, was on pace=
 to demand a five-year $345 million deal this
summer. Sure, the NBA=E2=80=99s $77 billion national media deals will kick =
in next season, but it=E2=80=99s hard to see how the Mavs could afford to k=
eep the star Slovenian guard while countenancing the dislocation of its loc=
al rights situation. There=E2=80=99s a growing belief inside professional s=
ports circles that Cuban, who sold the bulk of the team to the <strong>Adel=
son</strong> family last year, foresaw this scenario unfolding and cashed o=
ut in advance. Indeed, when Cuban bought the team in 2000, he helped the
NBA ownership understand the financial upside of their media potential. The=
 next wave of ownership economics will likely be defined by real estate dea=
lings=E2=80=94hotels, casino development, interactive stadiums, etcetera. I=
t=E2=80=99s hardly a wonder why Cubes is out and the Adelsons are in charge=
 now.</p></div>

50%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"margin:0;margi=
n-bottom:0px;">On a national level, media companies seem genuinely excited =
about the idea
that the Lakers, the league=E2=80=99s marquee team, will be led by a telege=
nic superstar after LeBron=E2=80=99s eventual retirement. <strong>Matt Kenn=
y</strong>, vice president of programming and acquisitions at ESPN, told me=
 that he was in Vegas for his daughter=E2=80=99s volleyball tournament when=
 he learned about the trade from his son back home. Naturally, Kenny immedi=
ately got up and checked the schedule to find out when the new-look Lakers =
would be on ESPN. (TNT has two games this week.) Kenny noted a March 8
game at the Celtics that will be on ABC and an April 9 game when Luka retur=
ns to Dallas.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p styl=
e=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">By Sunday morning, he had a summary typed=
 up: ESPN has eight more Mavs appearances for the rest of the season and se=
ven Lakers games, with an option to add an eighth that I presume he=E2=80=
=99ll pick up. Before the trade, Lakers national TV game ratings had been u=
p 1 percent year over year, including a 5 percent hike on ESPN.
=E2=80=9CThere=E2=80=99s a narrative out there, of course, that this cement=
s the Lakers stature in the league with an all-NBA dominant superstar for t=
he next decade,=E2=80=9D Kenny said. =E2=80=9CAs those of us in the busines=
s know, you just never know. But what this means in the short term is just =
an incredible shot in the arm for the NBA as it heads into All-Star weekend=
=2E And it certainly has a rising ti=
de effect on both teams.=E2=80=9D</p><p s=
tyle=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-=
bottom:0px;"><strong>Jared
Stacy</strong>, Prime=E2=80=99s director of global live sports production, =
told me he woke up to more than 100 texts. Perhaps because Amazon doesn=E2=
=80=99t begin its new NBA deal till next season, Stacy initially worried th=
at the inbound had to do with the fires. (Stacy, who lives in L.A., actuall=
y used to work for the Lakers.) =E2=80=9CThe first text I saw was a text fr=
om my cousin that simply said <em>WTF</em>,=E2=80=9D Stacy told me today. =
=E2=80=9CI start looking, and I see in my thread pictures of Luka in a Lake=
rs uniform, and
my mind pivots from terrible fire stuff to, wait=E2=80=A6 the Lakers got <e=
m>Luka</em>?=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p=
><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">He was excited for his team and h=
is city, of course, but also Amazon.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">=E2=80=9CThe=
 idea that one of the league=E2=80=99s great stars, at the age of 25, is co=
ming to L.A.? That=E2=80=99s pretty exciting for us,=E2=80=9D he said. =E2=
=80=9CAs I=E2=80=99m digesting it, I started to think that if we were on th=
e air right now, we would be able to go and
bring in Dirk Nowitzki for his thoughts. I started to think about how we wo=
uld cover it a year from now.=E2=80=9D</p></div>
